questions.text: Who are the Gujjar Bakarwals of Jammu and Kashmir?
questions.explanation: They are a pastoral community who move between their summer and winter grazing grounds.

questions.text: Where do the Gujjar Bakarwals spend their winters?
questions.explanation: In winter, when the high mountains are covered with snow, they live with their herds in the low hills.

questions.text: The Gaddi shepherds belong to which state?
questions.explanation: Gaddis are a pastoral community from Himachal Pradesh.

questions.text: What is a 'Bugyal'?
options: Vast meadows in the high mountains
questions.explanation: Bugyals are high-altitude summer grasslands in the Himalayas.

questions.text: Which community is an important pastoral community of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: Dhangars stay in the semi-arid central plateau of Maharashtra during the monsoon.

questions.text: During the monsoon, the central plateau of Maharashtra serves as a grazing ground for Dhangars because:
options: It is semi-arid and covered with poor grass
questions.explanation: The semi-arid nature prevents heavy cultivation, providing vast space for sheep.

questions.text: The Konkani peasants welcomed Dhangars because the sheep:
options: Manured the fields and ate the stubble
questions.explanation: Sheep droppings provide rich manure for the fields after the kharif harvest.

questions.text: The Gollas, Kurumas and Kurubas of Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh reared:
questions.explanation: Gollas herded cattle, while Kurumas and Kurubas reared sheep and goats.

questions.text: The Banjaras are found in which states of India?
questions.explanation: Banjaras were a well-known group of graziers found across several North and Central Indian states.

questions.text: The Raikas of Rajasthan combined pastoralism with:
questions.explanation: During the monsoons, the Raikas stayed in their home villages where they cultivated land.

questions.text: What happened to pastoral grazing lands under colonial rule?
options: They were converted into cultivated farms
questions.explanation: Colonial officials wanted to expand cultivation to increase land revenue and commercial crop production.

questions.text: Under the 'Waste Land Rules', uncultivated lands were seen as:
options: Unproductive and a loss of revenue
questions.explanation: The British saw uncultivated land as 'waste' because it produced neither revenue nor crops.

questions.text: The Forest Acts of the 19th century classified forests into:
questions.explanation: Reserved forests were totally closed to pastoralists, while in Protected forests, they needed permits.

questions.text: The 'Criminal Tribes Act' of 1871 targeted:
options: Communities of craftsmen, traders and pastoralists
questions.explanation: The British were suspicious of nomadic people and classified many such communities as 'Criminal Tribes' by birth.

questions.text: To expand its revenue, the colonial government imposed tax on:
questions.explanation: Taxes were imposed on almost everything, including every animal a pastoralist took to the forest.

questions.text: Between the 1850s and 1880s, the right to collect grazing tax was auctioned to:
questions.explanation: Contractors tried to extract the highest possible tax to recover the money they paid the state.

questions.text: How did pastoralists react when grazing lands shrank?
questions.explanation: They adapted to the new restrictions by changing their lifestyles and migration patterns.

questions.text: In 1947, the Raikas could no longer move into ______ because of the new political boundary.
questions.explanation: The partition of India cut off traditional grazing routes for many pastoralists.

questions.text: Where do over half of the world's pastoral population live?
questions.explanation: Over 22 million people in Africa depend on some form of pastoral activity.

questions.text: Which of these is an African pastoral community?
questions.explanation: Africa has a diverse range of pastoral communities living in varied environments.

questions.text: Before colonial rule, Maasailand stretched from north Kenya to the steppes of:
questions.explanation: The Maasai lived in a vast area in East Africa.

questions.text: In 1885, Maasailand was cut in half by an international boundary between:
questions.explanation: British Kenya and German Tanganyika (now Tanzania) divided the territory.

questions.text: What happened to the best grazing lands of the Maasai?
options: They were taken over for white settlement
They were given to other tribes
questions.explanation: The most fertile lands were cleared for European settlers, leaving Maasai with dry, poor land.

questions.text: Maasai Mara and Samburu National Park in Kenya were created from:
questions.explanation: Large areas of grazing land were converted into game reserves where pastoralists were not allowed.

questions.text: In Maasai society, who formed the ruling council and met to decide on tribal affairs?
questions.explanation: Elders decided on affairs and settled disputes within the community.

questions.text: The main role of 'Warriors' in Maasai society was to:
options: Protect the tribe and raid cattle
questions.explanation: Warriors proved their manhood by raiding cattle from other groups and defending their own.

questions.text: The British appointed 'chiefs' in Maasai society, which led to:
options: Wealth for the chiefs and loss of power for elders/warriors
questions.explanation: Chiefs accumulated wealth through trade and land, while poor pastoralists suffered.

questions.text: Pastoralists are often seen as 'backward' by modern states, but they are actually ______ to their environment.
questions.explanation: Pastoral nomadism is a sustainable way of living in dry and mountainous regions.

questions.text: Which season is particularly difficult for pastoralists?
questions.explanation: Drought kills animals and dries up grazing land, forcing large-scale migration or loss of stock.

questions.text: The word 'Maasai' is derived from 'Maa' which means:
questions.explanation: Maa-sai means 'My People'.

questions.text: What is 'stubble'?
options: Lower ends of grain stalks left in the ground after harvest
questions.explanation: Sheep feed on this stubble, which helps in clearing the field for the next crop.

questions.text: The dry season for Maasai meant they had to move to ______ regions.
questions.explanation: They traditionally moved to better-watered areas during dry spells.

questions.text: Nomadic people are those who:
options: Do not live in one place but move from one area to another to earn a living
questions.explanation: Movement is the central feature of nomadic life.

questions.text: The Bhotiyas and Sherpas are pastoralists found in:
questions.explanation: These communities live in the high mountains of the Himalayas.

questions.text: Kharif harvest usually takes place in:
questions.explanation: Kharif crops are sown with the monsoon and harvested in autumn.

questions.text: Rabi crops are harvested in:
questions.explanation: Rabi crops are winter crops harvested in the spring.

questions.text: Pastoralists in India and Africa today face challenges from:
questions.explanation: All these factors put pressure on traditional nomadic lifestyles.

questions.text: The 'Bhabar' is a dry forested area below the:
questions.explanation: Bhabar is the region at the foothills of the Himalayas where many pastoralists spend their winters.

questions.text: Under the British, Raikas who herded camels were known as:
questions.explanation: Maru Raikas specifically herded camels in the Thar desert.

questions.text: The mobility of pastoralists allows the ______ to recover.
questions.explanation: Moving from one area to another prevents overgrazing and lets the vegetation regrow.
